subjectLine: Thymus health predicts mortality — across 27,000 people 🫀
borisTake: The thymus paper is the one that changes how I think about immune aging — we've had thymic rejuvenation on the protocol radar for years, but this is the first large-scale human data showing thymic health directly tracks all-cause mortality and cancer incidence over 12 years. Combine this with the Rubedo GPX4 senolytic Phase 1 data and you're looking at a week where both the upstream (thymus, immunity) and downstream (senescent cell clearance) levers got serious clinical backing.
leadAnalysis: Researchers applied a deep learning framework to chest CT images from 27,612 adults across two independent cohorts (National Lung Screening Trial and Framingham Heart Study) to quantify thymic health non-invasively. Higher thymic health scores consistently associated with lower all-cause mortality, reduced lung cancer incidence, and lower cardiovascular mortality over 12 years — independent of age, sex, smoking, and comorbidities. The mechanism runs through T cell diversity: as the thymus atrophies, the adaptive immune system can't replace exhausted and senescent T cells fast enough, leaving the body increasingly unable to clear transformed or damaged cells. This repositions the thymus not as a vestigial organ after adolescence but as an active, modifiable regulator of immune-mediated disease susceptibility — making thymic regeneration strategies (GDF11, sex hormone modulation, IL-7 analogs) a much higher clinical priority than the field has historically treated them.
oneNumber: 27,612 | Adults across two cohorts where thymic health predicted mortality and cancer — over 12 years of follow-up

papers.title: Thymic Health Predicts All-Cause Mortality and Cancer Incidence in 27,000+ Adults
papers.summary: Deep learning analysis of chest CTs across two large prospective cohorts (NLST n=25,031, Framingham n=2,581) shows thymic health independently predicts all-cause mortality, lung cancer, and cardiovascular death over 12 years. Thymic health also correlates with systemic inflammation, metabolic dysregulation, and modifiable factors including smoking and obesity.

papers.title: Rubedo's RLS-1496 (GPX4 Senolytic) Shows Positive Phase 1 Results
papers.summary: First-ever GPX4 modulator in human trials completed Phase 1 in psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, and photo-aged skin. RLS-1496 was well-tolerated with no serious adverse events. Demonstrated dose-dependent reduction in senescent cells and inflammatory cytokines (IL-19, S100A7), 20% reduction in epidermal thickness in psoriasis patients, and early collagen upregulation in photo-aged skin via spatial transcriptomics.

papers.title: RNASEK Decline Allows Circular RNA Accumulation That Drives Aging
papers.summary: KAIST researchers show that RNASEK, the enzyme responsible for degrading circular RNA, declines with age in C. elegans and mammals. Circular RNA accumulation triggers toxic stress granule formation via HSP90 pathway disruption. Overexpression of RNASEK extends lifespan and healthspan — and the mechanism holds in human cell lines, making this a new causal aging mechanism rather than just a biomarker.

papers.title: Red Blood Cell Membrane-Encapsulated Mitochondria Rescue Parkinson's in Mice
papers.summary: Chinese researchers published in Cell describing a system where healthy mitochondria are encapsulated in erythrocyte plasma membranes, achieving 17x better brain delivery than standard LNPs. The system improved multiple Parkinson's models, restored ATP levels, and demonstrated human-compatible membrane material. Addresses the longstanding delivery barrier for mitochondrial therapy.

papers.title: LNP-Delivered TRIM11 mRNA Clears Tau Aggregates in Alzheimer's Mouse Models
papers.summary: Researchers in Cell Reports Medicine developed PLNP, an acetylcholine-mimicking lipid nanoparticle that crosses the blood-brain barrier and delivers mRNA encoding TRIM11 — an ATP-independent ligase that degrades tau aggregates. TRIM11 is downregulated in Alzheimer's. PLNP achieved near-17x greater mRNA delivery than conventional LNPs across all brain regions in wild-type mice, with clearance of tau aggregates in Alzheimer's models.

papers.title: Old-Microbiome Transfer Impairs Cognition in Young Mice via Vagus Nerve
papers.summary: Stanford researchers published in Nature showing co-housing young mice with old mice (causing microbiome transfer) impairs hippocampal memory on both NOR and Barnes maze tests. Antibiotic treatment reverses the effect. The mechanism is microbiome-vagus nerve signaling, identifying a specific bacterial species as the culprit. Physical frailty was unchanged, isolating the cognitive effect to microbial signals.

papers.title: 7-Ketocholesterol Hydroxylated Derivatives: New Toxic Oxysterol Class Mapped
papers.summary: Review expanding understanding of 7-ketocholesterol beyond a single toxic end-product: its downstream hydroxylated derivatives (7-keto-25-OHC and 7-keto-27-OHC) have distinct physicochemical properties — higher aqueous solubility, lower membrane affinity — that may amplify cytotoxic, pro-inflammatory signaling. Relevant to Cyclarity Therapeutics' clinical program and broader atherosclerosis/SENS damage-clearance strategy.
